docs.title: Vortex Odometry Library
docs.text: Welcome to the documentation for Vortex, a high-performance odometry and motion control library for VEX Robotics V5 using PROS.

docs.title: What's New in v2.0
docs.text:
• Arc Odometry — More accurate tracking during high-speed turns.
  • Pure Pursuit — Smooth path following with dynamic lookahead.
  • Trapezoidal Motion Profiling — Automatic smooth acceleration and deceleration.
  • IMU Drift Compensation — Corrects slow gyro creep while the robot is stationary.
  • Visual Telemetry — Live field map on the V5 brain screen with motor temperatures and battery.
  • Stall Detection — Automatically cancels motions when the robot is blocked.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Full 2-wheel + IMU and 3-wheel odometry
  • moveToPose, moveToPoint, turnToHeading, swingToHeading, follow
  • PID controllers with slew rate, windup protection, and settle logic
  • SD card CSV logging for post-match analysis
  • Built-in LLEMU auton selector
  • vortex-cli for version management and GitHub releases

docs.title: Get Started
docs.text:
1. Installation — Add Vortex to your PROS project
  2. Setup Guide — Configure motors and tracking wheels
  3. Tuning Parameters — Understand scrubFactor, trackWidth, and PID gains
  4. PID Tuning Guide — Use built-in tuning functions

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
void autonomous() {
    chassis.setPose(0, 0, 0);
    chassis.moveToPose(24, 24, 90, 4000);
    chassis.follow(my_path_txt, 15, 5000);
}

Vortex is open-source — GitHub

docs.title: API Overview
docs.text: All Vortex classes and functions live in the vortex:: namespace. Include "Vortex/api.hpp" to get everything.

docs.title: Core Classes
docs.text: Class Description Chassis Main robot controller — odometry, PID, and all motions Odom Arc odometry engine (managed by Chassis) TrackingWheel Encoder abstraction (Rotation, ADI, or Motor) PID Discrete PID controller ExitCondition Settle + timeout exit logic Timer Countdown timer with pause/resume AutonSelector LLEMU-based routine picker

docs.title: Configuration Structs
docs.text: Struct Description Drivetrain Motor groups, track width, RPM, drift, scrub ControllerSettings PID gains + exit condition thresholds OdomSensors Bundle of up to 4 tracking wheels + IMU MoveToPoseParams Per-call overrides for moveToPose MoveToPointParams Per-call overrides for moveToPoint TurnToHeadingParams Per-call overrides for turnToHeading TurnToPointParams Per-call overrides for turnToPoint SwingToHeadingParams Per-call overrides for swingToHeading

docs.title: Utility
docs.text: Function Description angleError Signed angular error with direction preference getCurvature Signed curvature between a pose and a target motionProfile Trapezoidal velocity profile slew Rate limiter ema Exponential moving average

docs.title: Enums
docs.text:
vortex::AngularDirection::AUTO            // shortest path (default)
vortex::AngularDirection::CW_CLOCKWISE
vortex::AngularDirection::CCW_COUNTERCLOCKWISE

vortex::DriveSide::LEFT
vortex::DriveSide::RIGHT

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text: See CHANGELOG.md on GitHub for the full history.

docs.title: Bug Fixes
docs.text:
Critical — Odometry X axis was inverted The global transform applied −cos(θ) and +sin(θ) on the lateral wheel term, which caused moving right to decrease X instead of increasing it and introduced phantom drift in every turn. The correct rotation matrix is x += localY·sin(θ) + localX·cos(θ).

Medium — turnToPoint double-converted angle error angleError(…, radians=false) already returns degrees, but the result was then passed through radToDeg() again — multiplying by 57.3 a second time and making every turn target ~57× too small.

Medium — Timer accumulated time on every getter call The original design added time − lastTime to timeWaited inside every call to getTimeLeft(), getTimePassed(), and isDone(). Calling any of them more than once per loop would accumulate extra time. Replaced with a start-time snapshot: elapsed = millis() − startTime, which is idempotent.

Medium — moveToPoint motion profiler saw a fluctuating total distance totalDist was recalculated every loop iteration. As the robot curves, the sum of currentDistToTarget + distTraveled fluctuates, making the profiler compute an unstable velocity ceiling. Now captured once before the loop starts.

Medium — follow() fell back to a stale lookahead point When no circle/segment intersection was found, the function reused lastLookaheadPoint — a stale value potentially from early in the path. Replaced with a forward-scanning closest-point fallback using a monotonically advancing segment index.

Low — getCurvature overflowed at θ=π/2 The original formula used tan(θ), which produces ±∞ when the robot faces exactly east or west. Rewrote using the full sin(θ)·x − cos(θ)·y line-equation form, which is finite at all headings.

Low — Float equality on horizontalDrift params.horizontalDrift == 0 used exact float comparison. Changed to < 1e-6.

CLI — Version bump never ran on plain invocation apply_version() was inside the if VERSION_CHANGED block, which was only set by the manual -v flag. Running ./vortex-cli always skipped it. Now apply_version() always runs before the release/dev split.

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• Timer fully rewritten with startTime snapshot — all getters are now idempotent.
  • getCurvature rewritten without tan().
  • follow() lookahead now scans forward monotonically — can't latch to past segments.
  • pros::Task in Buffer moved to a heap pointer — pros::Task has no default constructor.
  • mutex.take() calls throughout now pass TIMEOUT_MAX explicitly (PROS4 requirement).
  • pros::screen::print updated to pros::E_TEXT_MEDIUM enum (PROS4 naming).
  • Velocity estimation in odom.cpp moved from a function-local static to a class member — correct for multi-instance use.

docs.title: [2.0.0] — 2025
docs.text: • Initial arc odometry, Pure Pursuit, trapezoidal profiling, IMU drift compensation, SD logging, vortex-cli

docs.title: [1.x] — 2024
docs.text: • LemLib-derived odometry and PID chassis

docs.title: Drivetrain
docs.text:
vortex::Drivetrain drivetrain(
    &leftMotors,              // pros::MotorGroup*
    &rightMotors,             // pros::MotorGroup*
    10.0,                     // track width (inches)
    vortex::Omniwheel::NEW_4, // wheel diameter (inches)
    360,                      // output RPM
    2.0,                      // horizontal drift coefficient
    1.0                       // scrub factor (tune with calibratePhysical())
);

docs.title: ControllerSettings
docs.text:
Both lateral and angular controllers use the same struct.

vortex::ControllerSettings(kP, kI, kD, windupRange,
                            smallError, smallErrorTimeout,
                            largeError, largeErrorTimeout,
                            slew, maxVelocity=127, maxAccel=127);

docs.title: OdomSensors
docs.text:
vortex::OdomSensors sensors(&v1, &v2, &h1, &h2, &imu);

Any pointer can be nullptr. Heading priority: two tracking pods → IMU → motor encoders.

docs.title: Chassis
docs.text:
vortex::Chassis chassis(drivetrain, linearSettings, angularSettings, sensors);
// Optional: custom drive curves
vortex::Chassis chassis(drivetrain, linear, angular, sensors, &throttleCurve, &steerCurve);

docs.title: Optional Features
docs.text:
chassis.setStallDetection(10.0, 250);     // cancel if velocity < 10 RPM for 250 ms
chassis.setLogging(true, "/usd/log.csv"); // SD card telemetry at 10 Hz
chassis.setFieldRelative(true);           // arcade stick always points field-north
chassis.setImuDriftCompensation(true);    // correct IMU drift while stationary
chassis.setBrakeMode(pros::E_MOTOR_BRAKE_HOLD);

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• PROS 4.x or higher
  • A VEX V5 Brain
  • VS Code with the PROS extension (recommended)

docs.title: Method 1 — Manual ZIP (Recommended)
docs.text:
This method bypasses common PROS CLI kernel compatibility errors.

  1. Download Vortex.zip from the latest GitHub release.
  2. Move Vortex.zip into the root of your PROS project.
  3. Run in your project terminal:
pros c fetch Vortex.zip
pros c install Vortex

docs.title: Method 2 — Direct CLI
docs.text:
pros c fetch https://github.com/jonahchang207/Vortex/releases/download/v2.0.6/Vortex.zip
pros c install Vortex@2.0.6

If you see does not support kernel version, use Method 1 instead.

docs.title: Upgrading
docs.text: pros c upgrade Vortex

docs.title: Verify
docs.text:
Add to main.h:

#include "Vortex/api.hpp"

Then build:

make

A clean build with no errors means Vortex is installed correctly.

docs.title: Tuning Parameters
docs.text: This page explains every number you need to configure before your robot will move accurately.

docs.title: trackWidth
docs.text: The distance in inches between the contact patches of the left and right drive wheels — not the outside edge of the wheels, and not the robot frame. Measure from the center of each tire.

docs.title: wheelDiameter
docs.text:
The outer diameter of your drive wheels. Use the vortex::Omniwheel constants rather than measuring by hand — they account for real-world wear:

Constant Nominal Actual Omniwheel::NEW_275 2.75" 2.75" Omniwheel::NEW_4 4" 4.00" Omniwheel::OLD_4 4" 4.18" Omniwheel::NEW_325 3.25" 3.25"

docs.title: rpm
docs.text: Output shaft RPM after all external gearing. For a direct-drive blue cartridge: 600. For a 36:60 green cartridge: 200 × (36/60) = 120. For a common 36:60 blue: 600 × (36/60) = 360.

docs.title: horizontalDrift
docs.text: A dimensionless friction coefficient used to cap lateral speed on tight curves. Start with 2.0. Higher = robot can slide more freely on curves; lower = tighter lateral cap. Most competition wheels are 1.5–3.5.

docs.title: Odometry Offsets
docs.text: Each tracking wheel needs a signed offset from the robot's center of rotation:

docs.title: Vertical (parallel to drive) wheels
docs.text:
• Positive = left of center
  • Negative = right of center

docs.title: Horizontal (perpendicular to drive) wheels
docs.text:
• Positive = forward of center
  • Negative = behind center

To measure: place the robot on a flat surface and use calipers to measure from the robot's geometric center to the wheel contact point.

docs.title: scrubFactor
docs.text:
Accounts for wheel scrub (slip) during differential turns. Default is 1.0.

Run chassis.calibratePhysical() to get the correct value automatically. It spins the robot 10 turns and computes how much the tracking wheel actually traveled vs how much the IMU reported, then prints the suggested values to the terminal.

A scrubFactor > 1 means the robot under-turns (increase it). < 1 means over-turns (decrease it).

docs.title: kP (proportional)
docs.text: The main driving force. Start by setting kI and kD to 0, then increase kP until the robot reaches its target but oscillates slightly.

docs.title: kD (derivative)
docs.text: Dampens oscillation. Increase until oscillations stop. Too high and the robot becomes sluggish.

docs.title: kI (integral)
docs.text: Only add if the robot consistently stops a few inches short of the target. Keep it very small — start at 0.001. Use windupRange to prevent integral windup.

docs.title: windupRange
docs.text: The integral only accumulates when |error| is less than this. Prevents the integral from building up during large initial errors. Set to ~3× your smallError threshold.

docs.title: slew
docs.text: Maximum motor power change per 10 ms loop iteration. Prevents wheel slip on fast starts. Typical value: 15–25 for lateral, 0 for angular (turns usually don't need it).

docs.title: Exit Conditions
docs.text:
Two independent exit conditions per axis — the motion exits when either triggers.

Setting Meaning smallError Error threshold (in or deg) to be considered "arrived" smallErrorTimeout Must stay within smallError for this many ms before exiting largeError Looser threshold — triggers a hard timeout if stuck here too long largeErrorTimeout Hard timeout from the first loop iteration (prevents getting stuck forever)

Start with: smallError=1, smallErrorTimeout=100, largeError=3, largeErrorTimeout=500.

docs.title: 1. Physical Measurements
docs.text:
Measure your robot before writing any code.

Value Description Track Width Distance between left and right wheel centerlines (inches) Wheel Diameter Actual drive wheel diameter — use Omniwheel::NEW_4, NEW_275, etc. RPM Output shaft speed after gearing (e.g. 360 for a 36:60 blue cartridge) Horizontal Drift Lateral friction coefficient — start with 2.0 Tracking Wheel Offset Signed perpendicular distance from center of rotation to wheel contact (inches)

docs.title: Rotation Sensor (recommended)
docs.text:
pros::Rotation vertEnc(-11);  // negative port = physically reversed
vortex::TrackingWheel vertical(&vertEnc, vortex::Omniwheel::NEW_275, -2.5);
// 2.75" wheel, 2.5" to the right of center (negative = right)

docs.title: ADI Encoder
docs.text:
pros::adi::Encoder adiEnc('A', 'B');
vortex::TrackingWheel horizontal(&adiEnc, vortex::Omniwheel::NEW_275, -5.75);

docs.title: IMU
docs.text: pros::Imu imu(10);

docs.title: 3. OdomSensors
docs.text:
Pass nullptr for any sensor you don't have. Minimum: one vertical wheel + IMU, OR two vertical wheels.

vortex::OdomSensors sensors(
    &vertical,    // vertical tracking wheel 1  (required)
    nullptr,      // vertical tracking wheel 2  (optional)
    &horizontal,  // horizontal tracking wheel  (optional, improves strafe tracking)
    nullptr,      // second horizontal          (optional)
    &imu          // IMU                        (optional if two vertical pods)
);

docs.title: 4. ControllerSettings
docs.text:
vortex::ControllerSettings linear(
    10,   // kP — proportional gain
     0,   // kI — integral gain (use sparingly)
     3,   // kD — derivative gain
     3,   // windupRange — integral only accumulates within this error (inches)
     1,   // smallError — "settled" threshold (inches)
   100,   // smallErrorTimeout — ms to stay in smallError before exiting
     3,   // largeError — "close enough" threshold (inches)
   500,   // largeErrorTimeout — hard motion timeout (ms)
    20    // slew — max power change per 10 ms loop
);

docs.title: 5. Chassis
docs.text:
vortex::Chassis chassis(drivetrain, linear, angular, sensors);

void initialize() {
    chassis.calibrate(); // calibrates IMU, starts odometry + screen tasks
}

docs.title: AutonSelector
docs.text:
#include "Vortex/autonSelector.hpp"

LLEMU-based autonomous routine selector. Left button cycles backward, right cycles forward.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
vortex::AutonSelector selector;

void blueSafe() { /* ... */ }
void redSafe()  { /* ... */ }
void skills()   { /* ... */ }

void initialize() {
    chassis.calibrate();

    selector.addAuton("Blue Safe", blueSafe);
    selector.addAuton("Red Safe",  redSafe);
    selector.addAuton("Skills",    skills);
    selector.initialize(); // registers LLEMU button callbacks
}

void autonomous() {
    selector.run(); // calls whichever routine is selected
}

docs.title: addAuton(name, callback)
docs.text: Register a routine. Can be any std::function<void()> — lambda or function pointer.

docs.title: initialize()
docs.text: Sets up LLEMU display and button callbacks. Call at the end of initialize(). Only the first call has effect — safe to call from multiple places.

docs.title: run()
docs.text: Execute the currently selected routine. Call from autonomous().

docs.title: getSelectedName()
docs.text: Returns the name string of the currently selected routine.

docs.title: Display
docs.text:
The brain LCD shows: 

=== Vortex Auton Selector ===
> Blue Safe
  [1/3]

  < PREV           NEXT >

docs.title: Chassis
docs.text:
#include "Vortex/chassis/chassis.hpp"

The Chassis class is the top-level controller. It owns odometry, PID loops, background tasks, and every motion primitive.

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
vortex::Chassis chassis(drivetrain, linearSettings, angularSettings, sensors);
// With custom drive curves:
vortex::Chassis chassis(drivetrain, linear, angular, sensors, &throttleCurve, &steerCurve);

docs.title: calibrate(bool calibrateImu = true)
docs.text:
Calibrates the IMU (up to 5 retries), then starts background tasks: - Odometry (10 ms) - Brain screen telemetry (20 ms) - Stall detection (10 ms) - SD card logging (100 ms)

Call once in initialize().

docs.title: calibratePhysical()
docs.text: Spins the robot 10 full rotations and prints suggested scrubFactor and trackWidth. Run before PID tuning.

docs.title: setPose(float x, float y, float theta, bool radians = false)
docs.text: Set the robot's current position. Theta is degrees by default.

docs.title: getPose(bool radians = false, bool standardPos = false)
docs.text: Returns the current Pose. standardPos=true converts to math-standard CCW-from-east convention.

docs.title: Motions
docs.text: All motions accept an async flag. When true the function returns immediately and the motion runs in a background task.

docs.title: moveToPose(x, y, theta, timeout, params, async)
docs.text:
Drives to (x, y) and arrives facing theta degrees. Uses a carrot-point approach for smooth curves.

chassis.moveToPose(24, 24, 90, 4000);
chassis.moveToPose(-12, 0, 180, 3000, {.forwards=false, .lead=0.4});

docs.title: moveToPoint(x, y, timeout, params, async)
docs.text:
Drives to (x, y) without a final heading constraint. Uses a trapezoidal motion profile.

chassis.moveToPoint(24, 0, 2000);
chassis.moveToPoint(0, -24, 2000, {.forwards=false});

docs.title: turnThenMove(x, y, timeout, params, async)
docs.text: Turns to face (x, y) then drives straight to it. More accurate than moveToPoint when the robot starts badly misaligned.

docs.title: turnToHeading(heading, timeout, params, async)
docs.text:
Turns in-place to an absolute heading (degrees).

chassis.turnToHeading(90, 1500);
chassis.turnToHeading(270, 2000, {.direction=AngularDirection::CCW_COUNTERCLOCKWISE});

docs.title: turnToPoint(x, y, timeout, params, async)
docs.text: Turns in-place to face a field coordinate.

docs.title: swingToHeading(heading, side, timeout, params, async)
docs.text:
Turns with only one side of the drivetrain, pivoting around the locked wheel.

chassis.swingToHeading(90, vortex::DriveSide::LEFT, 1500);

docs.title: follow(path, lookahead, timeout, forwards, async)
docs.text:
Pure Pursuit path following. path is a ASSET() macro pointing to a CSV file in static/.

ASSET(my_path_txt); // static/my_path.txt
chassis.follow(my_path_txt, 15, 5000);

docs.title: tuneLateral(dist, timeout)
docs.text: Runs a lateral PID tuning motion and prints TUNE_LAT: time,error,power CSV to the terminal.

docs.title: tuneAngular(heading, timeout)
docs.text: Runs an angular PID tuning motion and prints TUNE_ANG: time,error,power CSV.

docs.title: Waiting
docs.text:
chassis.waitUntil(12);             // wait until 12 inches into the current motion
chassis.waitUntilPose(24, 24, 3);  // wait until within 3 inches of (24, 24)
chassis.waitUntilDone();           // wait until motion is fully complete
chassis.waitUntilDone(2000);       // same, but with a hard 2-second timeout

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
chassis.setBrakeMode(pros::E_MOTOR_BRAKE_HOLD);
chassis.setStallDetection(10.0, 250);       // cancel if < 10 RPM for 250 ms under load
chassis.setLogging(true, "/usd/log.csv");   // SD card telemetry
chassis.setFieldRelative(true);             // arcade always moves field-north
chassis.setImuDriftCompensation(true);      // correct slow gyro creep

docs.title: State
docs.text:
chassis.isInMotion()    // true during any autonomous motion
chassis.isStalled()     // true if stall was detected this motion
chassis.cancelMotion()  // abort the running motion
chassis.cancelAllMotions()

docs.title: Odometry
docs.text:
#include "Vortex/chassis/odom.hpp"

Odom runs in a 10 ms background task managed by Chassis. You don't construct it directly — it's owned by Chassis and configured via OdomSensors.

docs.title: Coordinate Convention
docs.text: Axis Direction +X East (right on field at θ=0) +Y North (forward at θ=0) θ=0 North θ increases Clockwise

docs.title: Heading Source Priority
docs.text:
1. Two dedicated (non-motor) vertical tracking wheels — differential measurement, most accurate
  2. IMU — single absolute heading, good if only one tracking pod
  3. Motor encoders — least accurate, only as a last resort

docs.title: Arc Integration
docs.text:
When the robot turns, Odom fits a circular arc to the encoder deltas and computes the chord displacement. This is more accurate than integrating straight-line segments, especially during fast pivots.

localX = 2·sin(Δθ/2) · (ΔH/Δθ + hOffset)
localY = 2·sin(Δθ/2) · (ΔV/Δθ + vOffset)

When Δθ ≈ 0 (straight line), simplifies to localX = ΔH, localY = ΔV.

docs.title: Global Transform
docs.text:
After computing local displacement:

pose.x += localY · sin(avgθ) + localX · cos(avgθ)
pose.y += localY · cos(avgθ) − localX · sin(avgθ)

where avgθ is the heading at the midpoint of the 10 ms interval.

docs.title: IMU Drift Compensation
docs.text:
When setDriftCompensation(true) is enabled, Odom monitors IMU heading while the robot is stationary (all encoder deltas < 0.001 in). It estimates the drift rate using an exponential moving average and subtracts one loop's worth of drift each update.

Enable with: 

chassis.setImuDriftCompensation(true);

docs.title: PID
docs.text:
#include "Vortex/pid.hpp"

Discrete PID controller. Called once per 10 ms loop.

vortex::PID pid(kP, kI, kD, windupRange, signFlipReset);
pid.reset();                   // clear integral and previous error
double output = pid.update(error); // feed error, get power output
  • windupRange: integral only accumulates when |error| < windupRange. Set to 0 to disable windowing.
  • signFlipReset: when true, resets the integral when error crosses zero. Prevents integral windup on approach.

docs.title: ExitCondition
docs.text:
#include "Vortex/exitcondition.hpp"

Determines when a motion is "done."

vortex::ExitCondition exit(
    1.0,   // error range (in or deg) to be considered "at target"
    100,   // settle time (ms): robot must stay in range for this long
    500    // maxTime (ms): hard timeout from first update() call
);

exit.reset();
exit.update(error);
if (exit.getExit()) { /* done */ }

Both conditions are independent — the motion exits when either triggers: 1. Error stays within range for settleTime ms 2. maxTime ms has passed since first update

docs.title: Timer
docs.text:
#include "Vortex/timer.hpp"

Countdown timer with pause/resume. All getters are idempotent — calling them multiple times in one loop does not accumulate extra time.

vortex::Timer t(1000);  // 1-second countdown

t.isDone()          // true when elapsed >= period
t.getTimeLeft()     // ms remaining
t.getTimePassed()   // ms elapsed
t.pause()           // freeze elapsed time
t.resume()          // continue from where paused
t.reset()           // restart countdown
t.waitUntilDone()   // blocking wait (5 ms poll)

docs.title: Tracking Wheels
docs.text:
#include "Vortex/chassis/trackingWheel.hpp"

TrackingWheel wraps any encoder type into a unified interface that returns inches traveled.

docs.title: pros::Rotation (Recommended)
docs.text:
36,000 centidegrees per revolution — highest resolution.

pros::Rotation enc(11);  // port 11, forward
pros::Rotation enc(-11); // port 11, reversed (hardware reversal)
vortex::TrackingWheel wheel(&enc, vortex::Omniwheel::NEW_275, -2.5);

docs.title: pros::adi::Encoder
docs.text:
360 ticks per revolution.

pros::adi::Encoder enc('A', 'B'); // ADI ports A and B
vortex::TrackingWheel wheel(&enc, vortex::Omniwheel::NEW_275, 5.0);

docs.title: pros::MotorGroup
docs.text:
Uses internal motor encoder in rotations mode. Least accurate — use only when no dedicated sensors are available.

vortex::TrackingWheel wheel(&leftMotors, vortex::Omniwheel::NEW_4, -5.0, 360.0);
//                                                                 offset  rpm

docs.title: Offset Sign Convention
docs.text:
• Vertical wheels (parallel to drive direction):
  • Positive offset = wheel is left of center

  • Negative offset = wheel is right of center

  • Horizontal wheels (perpendicular to drive):

  • Positive offset = wheel is forward of center
  • Negative offset = wheel is behind center

docs.title: Omniwheel Constants
docs.text:
vortex::Omniwheel::NEW_275   // 2.75" new omni
vortex::Omniwheel::NEW_4     // 4" new omni
vortex::Omniwheel::OLD_4     // 4" old omni (slightly larger)
vortex::Omniwheel::NEW_325   // 3.25" new omni
// ... see trackingWheel.hpp for full list

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
wheel.reset();                 // zero encoder position
wheel.getDistanceTraveled();   // total signed inches since last reset
wheel.getOffset();             // signed center-of-rotation offset

docs.title: Utility Functions
docs.text: #include "Vortex/util.hpp"

docs.title: angleError(target, position, radians, direction)
docs.text:
Signed angular error from position to target. Handles wrapping correctly.

double err = vortex::angleError(90.0, 350.0, false);
// Returns 100.0 (shortest path from 350° to 90° is +100° CCW)

double err = vortex::angleError(90.0, 350.0, false, AngularDirection::CW_CLOCKWISE);
// Returns -260.0 (forced CW route)

docs.title: degToRad(deg) / radToDeg(rad)
docs.text: Unit conversions. Constexpr.

docs.title: sanitizeAngle(angle, radians)
docs.text: Normalizes to [0, 2π) or [0, 360).

docs.title: normalizeAngle(angle)
docs.text: Normalizes to (−π, π].

docs.title: slew(target, current, maxStep)
docs.text:
Rate limiter. Limits how much current can change toward target in one step.

power = vortex::slew(targetPower, lastPower, 15.0); // max change 15/loop

docs.title: deadband(value, threshold)
docs.text: Returns 0 if |value| < threshold, otherwise returns value unchanged.

docs.title: ema(current, previous, smooth)
docs.text: Exponential moving average. smooth=1.0 → fully current; smooth=0.0 → fully previous.

docs.title: avg(values)
docs.text: Average of a std::vector<double>. Returns 0 for empty input.

docs.title: sgn(v)
docs.text: Returns +1 or -1. Works on any numeric type.

docs.title: getCurvature(pose, other)
docs.text: Signed curvature of the circle passing through pose (as a directed point) and other. Positive = turn left (CCW), negative = turn right (CW). Safe at all headings — does not use tan(θ).

docs.title: lineCircleIntersection(p1, p2, center, radius)
docs.text: Returns the intersection points of the segment p1→p2 with a circle. Returns empty if no intersection lies on the segment.

docs.title: motionProfile(dist, totalDist, maxVel, maxAccel, currentVel)
docs.text:
Trapezoidal velocity profile. Returns the velocity ceiling at position dist along a move of total length totalDist.

  • Accelerates from rest: v = sqrt(2·a·d)
  • Decelerates to rest: v = sqrt(2·a·(total − d))
  • Clamped to maxVel

docs.title: PID Tuning Guide
docs.text: Vortex has two built-in tuner functions that make PID tuning faster. They print CSV output to the PROS terminal so you can graph error and power over time.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Call the tuner from autonomous() — it takes over the chassis like a normal motion.

void autonomous() {
    chassis.setPose(0, 0, 0);
    chassis.tuneLateral(24);   // drive forward 24 inches
    // OR
    chassis.tuneAngular(90);   // turn to 90 degrees
}

Open the PROS terminal, then run the auto program. Copy the printed lines starting with TUNE_LAT: or TUNE_ANG: into a spreadsheet and plot columns 2 (error) and 3 (power) against column 1 (time ms).

docs.title: Lateral Tuning Steps
docs.text:
Start with kI=0 and kD=0 in your ControllerSettings.

  1. Set kP only. Increase it until the robot reaches the target but oscillates (rocks back and forth).
  2. Add kD. Increase until the oscillations stop. You want the robot to approach the target in one smooth motion without bouncing.
  3. Add kI only if needed. If the robot consistently stops 1–2 inches short of the target (steady-state error), add a small kI (try 0.001). Set windupRange to ~3× your smallError.
  4. Tune slew. If the robot spins its wheels on launch, increase slew. 15–25 is typical.

Signs of good lateral tuning: - Robot reaches target in ~1 second with no bounce - It stops cleanly — doesn't crawl forward and lock - Error on the graph reaches 0 and stays there

docs.title: Angular Tuning Steps
docs.text:
Same process. Use chassis.tuneAngular(90) to test a 90-degree turn.

  1. Set kP. Increase until the robot swings to the heading but oscillates.
  2. Add kD. Kills oscillation. Angular kD usually needs to be higher than lateral kD because inertia is high.
  3. kI is almost never needed for angular. Skip it unless you have consistent heading offset.
  4. slew is usually 0 for turns since you want full snap response.

Signs of good angular tuning: - Robot reaches heading in ~0.5 seconds with no oscillation - Final heading is within 1° of target

docs.title: Physical Calibration
docs.text:
Before tuning PID, get your scrubFactor and trackWidth right:

void autonomous() {
    chassis.calibratePhysical();
}

The robot spins 10 full rotations. The output gives you:

Suggested trackWidth (2-wheel):  10.24
Suggested scrubFactor:           0.98

Update Drivetrain with these values, then re-run until the printed values stabilize. Do PID tuning after this step — incorrect physical constants make PID gains meaningless.
